issues.issue: If the additionalProperties keyword is used it must be set to false.
issues.description: By default JSON Schema allows additional properties, which can potentially lead to mass assignment issues, where unspecified fields are passed to the API without validation. Disable them with `additionalProperties: false` or add `maxProperties`.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each flagged object schema, set `additionalProperties: false` after listing all expected properties, or model extensions via explicit named properties / `oneOf` instead of an open-ended object.

issues.issue: Declare intended audience of every server by defining servers[0].x-internal as true/false.
issues.description: Servers are required to use vendor extension x-internal set to true or false to explicitly explain the audience for the API, which will be picked up by most documentation tools.
issues.fixSuggestion: On every entry in `servers`, add `x-internal: true` (intranet-only, partners, VPN) or `x-internal: false` (public or external-facing) so tools and consumers know the intended audience.

issues.issue: Operation is missing rate limiting response in responses[429].
issues.description: OWASP API Security recommends defining schemas for all responses, even errors. A HTTP 429 response signals the API client is making too many requests, and will supply information about when to retry so that the client can back off calmly without everything breaking. Defining this response is important not just for documentation, but to empower contract testing to make sure the proper JSON structure is being returned instead of leaking implementation details in backtraces. It also ensures your API/framework/gateway actually has rate limiting set up.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each operation under `paths`, add a `429` response with a `content` entry (e.g. `application/json`) and a schema describing the rate-limit / retry body. Document retry semantics and reference gateway rate limits so clients and tests can rely on a stable contract.

issues.issue: Operation is missing responses[500].
issues.description: OWASP API Security recommends defining schemas for all responses, even errors. The 500 describes what happens when a request fails with an internal server error, so its important to define this not just for documentation, but to empower contract testing to make sure the proper JSON structure is being returned instead of leaking implementation details in backtraces.
issues.fixSuggestion: Add a `500` response on each operation with `content` and a schema for a generic server-error payload (avoid stack traces); reuse a shared component if all operations return the same error shape.

The 500 describes what happens when a request fails with an internal server error, so its important to define this not just for documentation, but to empower contract testing to make sure the proper JSON structure is being returned instead of leaking implementation details in backtraces.
issues.fixSuggestion: Add a `500` response on each operation with `content` and a schema for a generic server-error payload (avoid stack traces); reuse a shared component if all operations return the same error shape.

issues.issue: Missing error response of either 400, 422 or 4XX.
issues.description: Carefully define schemas for all the API responses, including either 400, 422 or 4XX responses which describe errors caused by invalid requests.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each operation, document at least one client-error response: `400` or `422` with a body schema, or a shared `4XX` response, so validation failures are part of the contract and testable.

issues.issue: Operation is missing rate limiting response in responses[429].
issues.description: OWASP API Security recommends defining schemas for all responses, even errors. A HTTP 429 response signals the API client is making too many requests, and will supply information about when to retry so that the client can back off calmly without everything breaking. Defining this response is important not just for documentation, but to empower contract testing to make sure the proper JSON structure is being returned instead of leaking implementation details in backtraces. It also ensures your API/framework/gateway actually has rate limiting set up.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each operation under `paths`, add a `429` response with a `content` entry (e.g. `application/json`) and a schema describing the rate-limit / retry body. Document retry semantics and reference gateway rate limits so clients and tests can rely on a stable contract.

issues.fixSuggestion: Add a `500` response on each operation with `content` and a schema for a generic server-error payload (avoid stack traces); reuse a shared component if all operations return the same error shape.

issues.issue: Missing error response of either 400, 422 or 4XX.
issues.description: Carefully define schemas for all the API responses, including either 400, 422 or 4XX responses which describe errors caused by invalid requests.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each operation, document at least one client-error response: `400` or `422` with a body schema, or a shared `4XX` response, so validation failures are part of the contract and testable.

issues.issue: Operation is missing rate limiting response in responses[429].
issues.description: OWASP API Security recommends defining schemas for all responses, even errors. A HTTP 429 response signals the API client is making too many requests, and will supply information about when to retry so that the client can back off calmly without everything breaking. Defining this response is important not just for documentation, but to empower contract testing to make sure the proper JSON structure is being returned instead of leaking implementation details in backtraces. It also ensures your API/framework/gateway actually has rate limiting set up.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each operation under `paths`, add a `429` response with a `content` entry (e.g. `application/json`) and a schema describing the rate-limit / retry body. Document retry semantics and reference gateway rate limits so clients and tests can rely on a stable contract.

issues.issue: Security schemes using JWTs must explicitly declare support for RFC8725 in the description.
issues.description:
JSON Web Tokens RFC7519 is a compact, URL-safe, means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. JWT can be enclosed in encrypted or signed tokens like JWS and JWE.

The [JOSE IANA registry](https://www.iana.org/assignments/jose/jose.xhtml) provides algorithms information.

RFC8725 describes common pitfalls in the JWx specifications and in
their implementations, such as:
- the ability to ignore algorithms, eg. `{"alg": "none"}`;
- using insecure algorithms like `RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5` eg. `{"alg": "RS256"}`.
An API using JWT should explicit in the `description`
that the implementation conforms to RFC8725.
```
components:
  securitySchemes:
    JWTBearer:
      type: http
      scheme: bearer
      bearerFormat: JWT
      description: |-
        A bearer token in the format of a JWS and conformato
        to the specifications included in RFC8725.
```
issues.fixSuggestion: On each affected OAuth2 or JWT bearer `securitySchemes` entry, set a non-empty `description` that explicitly states conformance to RFC 8725 (e.g. algorithm allowlists, signature validation, and rejection of "none" / weak algorithms).

issues.issue: If the additionalProperties keyword is used it must be set to false.
issues.description: By default JSON Schema allows additional properties, which can potentially lead to mass assignment issues, where unspecified fields are passed to the API without validation. Disable them with `additionalProperties: false` or add `maxProperties`.
issues.fixSuggestion: For each flagged object schema, set `additionalProperties: false` after listing all expected properties, or model extensions via explicit named properties / `oneOf` instead of an open-ended object.
